title: Sourcing first livestock: what to check before you buy
subtitle: The practical health checks and questions for buying stock from a reputable source.
excerpt: Buying livestock from the wrong source is the fastest way to introduce disease to a holding. This is a guide to the visible health checks, the questions to ask the seller, the registration requirements, and the quarantine approach when bringing any new animal onto a smallholding.
sourceNotes: DEFRA new keeper guidance for cattle, sheep, pigs, poultry, and goats. RSPCA welfare checks on livestock purchase. Farming Connect Wales, stock purchase checklist.

glossaryTerms.definition: A legally required period after moving certain livestock onto a holding during which other animals of that species cannot leave. For pigs in England this is 20 days; for sheep and goats 13 days. Check current APHA rules as periods change.

A document that must accompany certain livestock when moved between holdings. For cattle this is a cattle passport; for sheep and pigs a movement licence must be obtained from the seller before the animals leave.

content.text: New livestock from an unknown source is the most common route through which diseases enter small holdings. The time to check health and paperwork is before the animal comes home, not after. Most sellers with good stock expect these questions and can answer them without hesitation.
Health checks at point of purchase

Observe the animals in the group before selecting: are any individuals separate from the group, hunched, or disinterested in feed? For each animal you intend to buy: check that eyes are clear and bright, the coat or feathers are in reasonable condition, the animal is not lame, the abdomen is not bloated, and the animal is eating. In sheep and goats, check jaw condition; an animal that cannot hold weight with access to feed may have bad teeth. In pigs, check for skin lesions and persistent rubbing.

Ask specifically: what the animals are currently being fed, what vaccinations they have received and when, what worm treatment has been given and how recently, and whether any animals in the group have been treated for illness in the past month. A seller who hedges on any of these is not necessarily dishonest but is a reason for caution. Ask to see the medicine book.
Ensure you have your own CPH number registered with APHA before animals arrive. Obtain the correct
from the seller before transport. Understand the
that will apply: you may not be able to move other stock of the same species off the holding for 13 to 20 days. Plan the purchase timing accordingly.

Keep new arrivals separate from existing stock for a minimum of three to four weeks, even if they appear healthy. Many diseases are in incubation during transport stress and present within days of arrival. Treat for worms at the start of quarantine using a product from a different class than the seller used, to avoid resistance. Observe at least daily and contact your vet if anything changes.
